1: God spoke all these words — the Decalogue's divine origin is absolute. Jesus in the Sermon on the Mount claims equal authority with the Decalogue's Giver: “You have heard it said... but I say to you” (Matthew 5:21-48). He does not cite Torah as an authority above himself but speaks as the one through whom Torah was originally given — the Word who was with God in the beginning (John 1:1-3).
2: “I am YHWH your God who brought you out of Egypt, out of the house of slavery.” The gospel-before-law prologue: the commands follow from the prior act of grace. The same structure governs the NT: Christ's redemption (the new Exodus, Luke 9:31) grounds all subsequent ethical demands. Romans 12:1 — “therefore, in view of God's mercy” — is the NT equivalent of this prologue: imperatives follow from indicatives.
3: “You shall have no other gods beside me.” Christ fulfills this commandment perfectly: he has no competing allegiance, no divided loyalty. He is the one who perfectly answers “Yes” to all of YHWH's demands (2 Corinthians 1:20). His active obedience — the perfect keeping of every commandment — is counted as righteousness for those who trust in him (Romans 5:19; 2 Corinthians 5:21).
4: “You shall not make for yourself an idol in the form of anything.” Christ is the one legitimate image (eikon) of the invisible God (Colossians 1:15; 2 Corinthians 4:4; Hebrews 1:3). The prohibition of human-made images is not overturned by the Incarnation but fulfilled by it: YHWH himself provides the image that human hands may not make. Jesus is the image the second commandment forbids humans from manufacturing and that YHWH himself gives.
5: “I am a jealous God.” YHWH's jealousy is covenantal-marital exclusivity — the same passion that drives the new covenant. Paul uses the same jealousy-language for Christ and the church: “I am jealous for you with a godly jealousy. I promised you to one husband, to Christ, so that I might present you as a pure virgin to him” (2 Corinthians 11:2). The jealous God who forbids idols is Christ who will not share his bride with rivals.
6: “Showing faithful love (chesed) to thousands who love me and keep my commandments.” The covenant loyalty YHWH displays to thousands is supremely enacted in Christ's death: “God demonstrates his own love for us in this: while we were still sinners, Christ died for us” (Romans 5:8). The chesed promised in the Decalogue is fulfilled in the cross.
7: “You shall not misuse the name of YHWH your God.” Jesus came in the Father's name (John 5:43; Exodus 10:25) and revealed the Father's name to his disciples (John 17:6, 26). He glorified the Father's name throughout his ministry (John 12:28; Exodus 17:1-4). The negative prohibition on misusing the name finds its positive fulfillment in Christ who hallowed it perfectly.
8: “Remember the Sabbath day by keeping it holy.” Jesus is “Lord of the Sabbath” (Matthew 12:8; Mark 2:28; Luke 6:5) — the Sabbath was made for humanity, and he is its Lord and fulfillment. Matthew 11:28-30 — “come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest” — is Jesus's invitation to the Sabbath rest he embodies. Hebrews 4:9-11 teaches that the true Sabbath-rest is entered by faith in Christ's finished work.
9: Six days of labor — the work-week is affirmed as divine institution. Christ himself worked: “My Father is always at his work to this very day, and I too am working” (John 5:17). The six-day work structure is not abolished but fulfilled in the new creation's week, which begins with the Sabbath-day resurrection (Matthew 28:1; John 20:1 — the first day of the new creation week).
10: The seventh day is a Sabbath to YHWH — no work for anyone in the household, including servants and animals. The inclusive Sabbath rest — extending to every member of the household including foreigners and animals — is the type of the eschatological rest that encompasses all creation (Romans 8:21) and all peoples (Revelation 7:9-10). Christ's rest is inclusive: “Come to me, all you who are weary” (Matthew 11:28) — no exclusions.
11: “For in six days YHWH made the heavens and the earth... but he rested on the seventh day.” Creation Sabbath grounds the weekly Sabbath. Christ's rest in the tomb on the seventh day (Luke 23:54-56; Mark 15:42-47) is the fulfillment of creation's Sabbath: the one through whom all things were made (John 1:3; Colossians 1:16) rests in death on the seventh day, completing the work of new creation. The first day (Sunday) begins the new creation week with resurrection.
12: “Honor your father and your mother.” Jesus honored his mother (John 19:26-27) and submitted to his earthly parents (Luke 2:51-52). He also set the fifth commandment in its proper order: “Whoever loves father or mother more than me is not worthy of me” (Matthew 10:37) — fulfilling the commandment while locating the supreme loyalty it ultimately requires. In John 19:26-27, he fulfills the commandment's care-of-parents dimension from the cross itself.
13: “You shall not murder.” Jesus deepens this commandment to the heart level: anger and contempt are murder's root (Matthew 5:21-22). He himself is the innocent one murdered by those who fulfilled the worst logic of this commandment's violation (Acts 2:23; Exodus 3:15). His death at human hands, combined with his forgiveness of those hands (“Father, forgive them,” Luke 23:34), is the commandment's ultimate exposure and the grace that transcends it.
14: “You shall not commit adultery.” Jesus deepens this to the level of desire (Matthew 5:27-28). He is the faithful husband-figure (Mark 2:19-20; John 3:29) whose covenant fidelity is the standard for marital faithfulness. Ephesians 5:25-32 makes the husband-wife relationship a type of Christ-church: the absolute faithfulness of Christ to his bride defines what the commandment's fulfillment looks like.
15: “You shall not steal.” Christ “emptied himself” (Philippians 2:6-7) and “did not regard equality with God as something to be grasped.” He fulfills the commandment by giving rather than taking — his entire ministry is the giving of what is his for the benefit of others. Ephesians 4:28 applies this positively: the one who was stealing must instead work to give to those in need, reflecting Christ's own economy of giving.
16: “You shall not bear false testimony against your neighbor.” Christ is “the truth” (John 14:6) — the one whose every word is true (John 8:45-46). At his trial he bore the false testimony of others (Mark 14:56-59) while himself bearing only true witness to the Father (Mark 14:62; John 18:37: “for this I came into the world, to testify to the truth”). The ninth commandment finds its positive fulfillment in the one who is Truth incarnate.
17: “You shall not covet.” Paul in Romans 7:7-8 identifies this as the commandment that most clearly revealed sin in him: “I would not have known what coveting really was if the law had not said, ‘Do not covet.’” Christ fulfills this by contentment and generosity: “Godliness with contentment is great gain” (1 Timothy 6:6) traces to Christ's own disposition of trusting the Father's provision rather than grasping.
18: The people saw the thunder and lightning and the mountain in smoke — they trembled and stood at a distance. Hebrews 12:18-21 recounts this terror of Sinai in contrast to the boldness of the new covenant: the old covenant's encounter produced fear and distance; the new covenant's encounter through Christ's blood produces confidence and access (Hebrews 10:19; Exodus 4:16). The fear at Sinai is not wrong — it is accurate perception of divine holiness — but it is incomplete without the mediator who has satisfied that holiness.
19: “Speak to us yourself and we will listen. But do not have God speak to us or we will die.” The people's request for a human mediator — they cannot endure direct divine speech — is the OT expression of the Incarnation's necessity. The people need a human voice to mediate the divine word. Christ is the answer: the divine Word in human voice (John 1:14; Hebrews 1:1-2). The one through whom YHWH speaks in these last days is himself both fully divine and fully human — the mediator the people at Sinai needed.
20: “God has come to test you, so that the fear of God will be with you, that you may not sin.” The purpose of the terrifying theophany: holy fear preventing sin. Christ accomplishes this purpose by a different path — not terror but love (1 John 4:18-19: “perfect love drives out fear... we love because he first loved us”) while maintaining the ultimate seriousness of divine holiness: “It is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God” (Hebrews 10:31).
21: Moses approached the thick darkness where God was — the people remained at a distance. Moses enters the cloud of divine presence; the people cannot. Christ is the new Moses who enters not into darkness but into the Father's light (1 Timothy 6:16), and through his mediation draws his people into that same presence — not to the foot of the mountain but into the very holy of holies (Hebrews 10:19-22; John 14:3: “I will come back and take you to be with me”).
22: YHWH's first post-Decalogue instruction: “You have seen that I have spoken to you from heaven — you shall not make gods of silver or gold.” The prohibition against silver and gold gods follows immediately after the Decalogue. The Incarnation is YHWH's definitive self-revelation that replaces all human-made representations: “The Son is the radiance of God's glory and the exact representation of his being” (Hebrews 1:3).
23: “Do not make alongside me gods of silver or gods of gold.” The restatement of the second commandment applied to the worship context. Christ is the one alongside whom no other god stands — all things were created through him and for him (Colossians 1:16), and he is the image of the invisible God (Colossians 1:15). No silver or gold image captures what the Incarnation reveals; no human-made god competes with the one the Father sent.
24: The earthen altar — simplicity of approach before YHWH. The first altar instruction emphasizes accessibility over architecture: an earthen altar, anywhere YHWH causes his name to be remembered. The simplicity contrasts with the elaborate tabernacle and points forward to Christ's own statement: “Where two or three gather in my name, there am I with them” (Matthew 18:20). The presence of YHWH among his people is not dependent on elaborate structure.
25: If the altar is of stone, it must be unhewn — the chisel desecrates it. The principle: YHWH's altar must not bear human marks of improvement. The same principle governs the new covenant: Christ is the cornerstone and foundation that no human hand shapes (Acts 4:11; 1 Peter 2:4-6). His sacrifice needs no human supplement or improvement; any addition desecrates rather than improves.
26: “Do not go up to my altar by steps, so your nakedness will not be exposed.” The altar must preserve human dignity before YHWH — no steps that would expose nakedness. The principle of covered approach is the type of the righteousness-covering that enables approach to God: Christ's righteousness covers the believer's shame (Galatians 3:27; Revelation 3:18), making approach to God possible without the exposure of unworthiness.
